summary:
Nalanda University (founded c. 5th century AD, possibly under Kumaragupta I) was the world's first residential university and the greatest center of Buddhist learning in Asia — a vast monastic-academic complex in Bihar (northeastern India) that at its peak housed over 10,000 students and 2,000 teachers from across Asia, studying not just Buddhist philosophy and scripture but also logic, grammar, medicine, mathematics, and other subjects. Nalanda attracted scholars and pilgrims from China, Korea, Japan, Tibet, Mongolia, Ceylon, and Southeast Asia, functioning as a cosmopolitan intellectual hub for over 700 years.

The university complex included multiple colleges, libraries (reportedly containing hundreds of thousands of manuscripts in a multi-story building called the Dharmagañja), meditation halls, dormitories, and temples. Admission was competitive and highly selective — Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang, who studied there in the 7th century, described rigorous entrance examinations and a faculty of renowned scholars. The subjects taught ranged from Mahayana and Theravada Buddhist philosophy to Vedic scriptures, logic, grammar, astronomy, and medicine. Nalanda was the institutional engine through which Indian Buddhist thought was transmitted to East Asia — its graduates carried texts and translations back to China, Korea, and Tibet. The university was destroyed by Bakhtiyar Khilji's Muslim forces around 1193 AD, burning its libraries in a catastrophe for world scholarship, though some scholarly traditions managed to survive in Tibet.
